text: The surgeon frees the inferior pole of the spleen from adhesions and ligates the inferior polar vessels. Subsequently, the superior pole is freed by initially ligating the superior short gastric vessels, followed by the complete takedown of the remaining short gastric vessels and superior polar vessels.
The surgeon dissects the splenic hilum, isolating both the splenic artery and vein. The splenic artery is observed to have a magistral distribution, dividing near the hilum.

The surgeon uses a vascular stapler device to transect each vessel separately, ensuring good hemostasis while carefully avoiding injury to the pancreas during dissection in the hilar area. The spleen is then mobilized by freeing its attachments, including the splenocolic, splenorenal, and splenophrenic ligaments.

The splenic mass is visible during the maneuver and is carefully protected from injury. After the spleen is fully mobilized, it is placed into an endobag for extraction following partial marsupialization. Minimal irrigation is performed, and hemostasis is confirmed.
The surgeon uses a zero-vicryl suture to close the fascia of the left subcostal incision.
